english: clouds, frq. water, *kói kă-ču kor* (kho'i kha chu khor) W. it makes his mouth water; dgrá-bo °kor (dgra bo 'khor) Mil. enemies are collecting (we create ourselves enemies); also impersonally: *°kór soñ* ('khor song) it has become cloudy; rnam °kor (rnam 'khor) the sky is getting overcast, clouded; therefore even to arise, to be produced, formed, zil-pa °kor (zil pa 'khor) dew is produced, ryá (rya), rust, even: lus-la sras °kor (lus la sras 'khor) a child has been formed, produced, in the womb Pth. — II. sbst. 1. the turning round or about etc.; more particularly 2. the orb or round of transmigration within the six classes of physical beings. Though the Buddhist has not a more ardent wish, than to be finally released from the repeated wanderings of the soul, yet he believes so firmly in these migrations, that he will rather follow the doctrines of his philosophers, and doubt the reality of the perception of his senses, than think it possible, that the whole theory of the °kór-ba ('khor ba) with all its consequences should be nothing but a product of human imagination. — °kór-bar °kór-ba ('khor bar 'khor ba) to turn round, to wander about in the orb of transmigration; °kór-bai btsón-ra ('khor ba'i btson ra), °dam ('dam), mtśo (mtsho) the dungeon, the swamp, the sea of the °kór-ba ('khor ba); °kór-ba-las °dás-pa ('khor ba las 'das pa) to escape from the °kór-ba ('khor ba), = to enter into the Nirwana; °kór-°das ('khor 'das) 1. abbreviation of the foregoing. 2. for °kór-ba dań °das-pa ('khor ba dang 'das pa) the stay in the °kór-ba ('khor ba) and the escape from it; °kor °das rnyís-su ńas ma mtóń ('khor 'das gnyis su ngas ma mthong) I have not seen that there is a difference between these two Mil.
a wall, rampart Cs., v. kó-ra (kho ra).

(Ssk. चक्र, मण्डल) 1. circle, tsógs-kyi °kór-lo (tshogs kyi 'khor lo) offerings arranged in a circle, v. tsogs (tshogs); °kór-lo brí-ba ('khor lo bri ba) to describe a circle Tar. More frq.: 2. a circular body, a disk, roll, wheel, any modification of the cylinder, bču-gsum-°kór-lo (bcu gsum 'khor lo) the column on the mčod-rtén (mchod rten) consisting apparently of thirteen circular disks; °kór-lo brtsib-brgyád ('khor lo brtsib brgyad) the wheel with eight spokes, a frq. attribute of deities; rdza-mkán-gyi °kór-lo (rdza mkhan gyi 'khor lo) potter's wheel; čós-kyi °kór-lo (chos kyi 'khor lo) praying-cylinder, cf. below; also a complication of wheels, wheel-work, engine, °prúl-(gyi) °kór(-lo) ('phrul (gyi) 'khor (lo)) 'magic wheel', a phantastic attribute of gods, but also any real machine of a more ingenious construction, e.g. sugar-press Stg., electrical machine etc.: čú-tsod-°kór-lo (chu tshod 'khor lo) a clock; śiń-rta-°kór-lo (shing rta 'khor lo) waggon, carriage, also cart-wheel. — Figuratively: bdé-ba dań sdug-bsńál-gyi °kór-lo (bde ba dang sdug bsngal gyi 'khor lo), vicissitude of fortune; dús-kyi °kór-lo (dus kyi 'khor lo) (कालचक्र) acc. to Cs.'s Chronological Table (Cs.'s Gram. p. 181) a later philosophical system, contained esp. in the rtsa-rgyúd (rtsa rgyud), Mūlatantra, in which the Adibuddha doctrine, prophecies, chronology etc. are propounded. It was introduced into Tibet about 1000 p. Chr.; cf. also Schl. 45. — °kór-lo sgyúr-ba ('khor lo sgyur ba), or skór-ba (skor ba), with čós-kyi (chos kyi), to turn the wheel of doctrine, = to preach, to teach religion, (vulgo understood only of the turning of the praying-cylinder); *čó-kyi kór-lo léń-mor be'-pa* (cho kyi khor lo leng mor be' pa) C. to devote one's self to the preaching of religion. On the other hand: °kór-los sgyúr-bai rgyál-po ('khor los sgyur ba'i rgyal po) (चक्रवर्तिन्) Will.: 'a ruler, the wheels of whose chariot roll everywhere without obstruction, emperor, sovereign of the world, the ruler of a ča-kra (cha kra), or country extending from sea to sea'. In this Indian explanation two different etymologies are given, the former of which is undoubtedly the original one. Buddhism and the Tibetan language have added a third signification, 'praying-wheel'; modern scholars a fourth, that of the 'orb' or round of transmigration or metempsychosis: hence the confusion attaching to the import of this word.
*°kor-lóg* ('khor log) is said to be used in col. language instead of ka-ló (kha lo) 3. W.
= skór-lam (skor lam) v. skór-ba (skor ba) I. extr.; °kór-sa bár-pa ('khor sa bar pa), čén-po (chen po) Glr.
lit. three circles, Ssk. trimaṇḍala; Sch.: 'every thing that belongs to archery'; more correctly: arrow, knife, and spear.
